summary: Joe Reis argues that AI should be compared to electrification rather than the dot-com bubble, drawing on Paul David's research showing that electric motors took 40 years to boost productivity because factories kept the old steam-era layouts. He contends that enterprises today are making the same mistake—bolting AI onto existing architectures and org structures instead of fundamentally redesigning how work gets done.
key_points: The dot-com analogy for AI is wrong; electrification's 40-year productivity lag is more apt because the bottleneck was organizational redesign, not technology
Enterprises are repeating the 'motor swap' pattern: bolting AI onto existing architectures (CoPilot on SharePoint, chatbots on warehouses) without changing org design or workflows
The data industry has done this repeatedly with cloud migration, Hadoop, and the modern data stack—shinier tools, same centralized factory layout
Using AI to accelerate code migrations is the most dangerous trap because it feels like progress while encoding old architectural assumptions into new code
Real transformation will take 10-20 years, not 3-5, and winners will likely be new entrants who build from scratch rather than incumbents
money_quotes: A CoPilot subscription doesn't magically transform you into an AI company.
You've paved the cow path with better asphalt. But it's still a cow path.
We're in 1905. The electric motor works, and everyone's bought one. Almost nobody has redesigned the factory to fully optimize the motor's potential.
At some point, you've got to look in the mirror and ask: Is it the tech, or is it us? I think it's us. It's always been us.
That's the 2026 equivalent of bolting an electric motor onto your steam engine and declaring you've electrified the factory.
key_insight: AI's productivity gains won't come from adopting the technology itself but from fundamentally redesigning organizational structures and workflows around it—a transformation that history suggests will take decades, not years.
